Arguments.prompt: Query the USGS Earthquake API at https://earthquake.usgs.gov/fdsnws/event/1/query with the following parameters: format=geojson, a time window covering the past 30 days from today (compute starttime as today minus 30 days and endtime as today, both formatted as YYYY-MM-DD), minmagnitude=2.0, minlatitude=32, maxlatitude=49, minlongitude=-125, maxlongitude=-114. Parse the GeoJSON response and extract individual earthquake feature records. For each feature, extract the following fields: magnitude (from properties.mag), depth in kilometers (from geometry.coordinates index 2), latitude (from geometry.coordinates index 1), and longitude (from geometry.coordinates index 0). Assemble these into a tabular dataset with columns named magnitude, depth, latitude, and longitude. Drop any rows where magnitude or depth is null or missing, as both fields are required for visualization. Also record the query start date, query end date, and the total count of records remaining after filtering. Save the cleaned earthquake dataset as a CSV file named earthquake_data.csv. Save a small metadata JSON file named earthquake_meta.json containing the query start date string, query end date string, and the filtered event count under keys start_date, end_date, and event_count respectively.

Download the US Census Bureau cartographic boundary shapefile for US states at the 20-million scale from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip. Unzip the archive and load the shapefile as a GeoDataFrame. Filter the GeoDataFrame to retain only the following eight states by their NAME field: California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Idaho, Arizona, Utah, and Montana. Save the filtered GeoDataFrame as a GeoJSON file named state_boundaries.geojson. This file will be used in the next step to render state boundary lines on the earthquake map.

Load the earthquake dataset from the CSV file produced by the data-fetching step (columns: magnitude, depth, latitude, longitude). Load the metadata JSON file produced by the same step to retrieve the query start date string, query end date string, and total filtered event count. Load the state boundaries GeoJSON file produced by the boundary-fetching step. Using these inputs, produce a styled earthquake map as follows. Compute marker sizes using an exponential scale: size = 80 * (2 ** (magnitude - min_magnitude)), where min_magnitude is the minimum magnitude value present in the dataset. Map each earthquake's depth value to the plasma colormap using matplotlib's automatic normalization based on the minimum and maximum depth values in the dataset. Create a matplotlib figure of size 12 by 8 inches. Set the figure facecolor and the axes facecolor both to the dark navy hex color #0d1b2a. Plot the state boundary geometries from the loaded GeoDataFrame onto the axes, rendering boundary lines in light gray hex color #cccccc at linewidth 0.8 with no fill (facecolor set to none). Overlay a dashed grid in light gray with alpha 0.3 and linewidth 0.5. Plot the earthquake locations as a scatter plot using the computed exponential marker sizes, the plasma colormap applied to depth values with the same normalization, and black edge colors at linewidth 0.3. Set the axes aspect to equal. Set the x-axis limits to -125 and -114 and the y-axis limits to 32 and 49. Add a colorbar to the figure and label it 'Depth (km)'. Add a legend positioned outside the map to the right using loc='upper left' and bbox_to_anchor=(1.15, 1.0). The legend should display representative scatter handles for magnitudes M2, M3, M4, M5, and M6, with each handle sized using the same exponential formula (size = 80 * (2 ** (M - min_magnitude)) where M is the representative magnitude value), markerscale=0.4, labelspacing=1.2, and handletextpad=1.0. Set the legend facecolor to #0d1b2a and legend text color to white. Set the figure title as a single line formatted as: 'Western US Earthquakes (M2.0+) | <start_date> to <end_date> | <N> events', substituting the actual start date, end date, and filtered event count from the metadata. Set the title color to white. Set axis tick label colors to white and axis label colors to white. Save the completed figure as a PNG file named earthquake_map.png using tight bounding box layout to ensure the legend and colorbar are not clipped.
